metadata.title: Computer science reasoning graph (gold, true summary semantics)
metadata.source_note: Designed for controller supervision with atomic CS/logic claims, branchy support, explicit misconceptions, cross-cluster bridges, and reserved summary semantics for component compression.

relation_convention.part_of: child hub or bridge node -> parent hub
relation_convention.support: evidence, mechanism, prerequisite, or refinement -> claim
relation_convention.contradict: accepted or resolving claim -> false or competing claim

notes: Hub nodes are structural containers only and are not summary nodes.
Summary nodes are reserved for compressed component-level abstractions created only when a component can be sparsified into one reusable language node.
Ordinary claims are grouped by metadata cluster rather than by attaching every statement to a topic sentence.
False statements are intentionally retained with low confidence because they provide resolve_conflict and update_node supervision.
Branching is preferred over narrow chains: multiple supports, examples, and consequences are included where possible.
Bridge nodes connect logic, algorithms, computation, programming, and systems into reusable reasoning abstractions.

nodes.text: Computer science and logic study computation, reasoning, and formal systems.
Logic and reasoning study valid inference, proof, and formal language.
Algorithms study step-by-step problem solving, correctness, and efficiency.
Theory of computation studies computability, reductions, and complexity classes.
Programming studies program structure, types, testing, and implementation behavior.
Systems study concurrency, resource coordination, and performance behavior.
Bridge insights connect logic and computer science to verification, hardware, and security.
A valid argument preserves truth from premises to conclusion in every case.
Propositional logic combines statements with connectives such as AND, OR, and NOT.
Predicate logic adds variables and quantifiers such as ∀ and ∃.
From P and P→Q, modus ponens infers Q.
Proof by contradiction derives impossibility by assuming the opposite of the target claim.
Mathematical induction proves a statement by establishing a base case and an inductive step.
A single counterexample disproves a universal claim.
A few successful examples are enough to prove a universal statement.
A loop invariant is a property that remains true before and after each iteration.
Binary search requires the searchable sequence to be sorted.
Binary search is correct when its invariant is maintained on a sorted sequence.
Each binary search step discards half of the remaining search interval.
Big-O notation bounds asymptotic growth up to constant factors.
Binary search runs in O(log n) comparisons.
Greedy optimality requires a problem-specific justification such as a greedy-choice or exchange argument.
A greedy algorithm always produces an optimal solution.
Activity selection is a classic problem where a greedy strategy is optimal.
A Turing machine is a standard abstract model of algorithmic computation.
No algorithm decides for every program and input whether that program halts.
A polynomial-time reduction transforms one problem into another while preserving efficient solvability implications.
Boolean satisfiability is NP-complete.
A problem can be decidable yet still be computationally infeasible at large scale.
Every computable problem can be solved efficiently.
Static type checking can rule out some classes of program error before execution.
Testing can reveal bugs and increase confidence, but it does not generally prove correctness.
If a program passes many tests, it is proven correct.
A recursive definition needs a valid base case to avoid infinite descent.
Concurrent execution introduces multiple possible interleavings of operations.
Deadlock requires mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, and circular wait.
Dining philosophers is a standard example of potential deadlock.
Cache locality can strongly affect real program performance even when asymptotic complexity is unchanged.
Synchronization, communication, and contention can limit parallel speedup.
Parallelism always makes a program faster.
Boolean logic provides the conceptual basis for digital circuit design.
Predicate logic underlies formal specification and program verification.
Induction mirrors recursion and is central to proving recursive program correctness.
Modern cryptographic practice relies on problems believed to resist efficient solution.
Swapping quantifier order can change the meaning and truth conditions of a statement.
The order of universal and existential quantifiers does not affect meaning.
The statements 'every process has some owner' and 'some owner owns every process' are not equivalent, illustrating quantifier-order sensitivity.
Loop-invariant proofs establish correctness by showing initialization, maintenance, and termination.
Binary search terminates because each step strictly shrinks the remaining candidate interval until a match is found or the interval becomes empty.
Breadth-first search finds shortest paths in an unweighted graph because it explores vertices in nondecreasing distance layers.
A maze modeled as an unweighted graph is a standard example where breadth-first search returns a shortest path in number of steps.
Dynamic programming is justified when subproblems overlap and optimal solutions can be composed from optimal subproblem solutions.
A decision problem is in NP if proposed solutions can be checked in polynomial time by a verifier.
Every problem in P is also in NP because a polynomial-time solver can serve as a polynomial-time verifier.

Computational hardness is different from undecidability: some hard problems are still decidable, while some undecidable problems are not merely hard but impossible to decide algorithmically in full generality.
If candidate solutions can be verified quickly, then the problem must also be easy to solve quickly.
Static typing and testing are complementary: types rule out some classes of mistakes, while tests probe behaviors not guaranteed by the type system alone.
A static type system eliminates all meaningful program bugs.
Recursive program correctness is commonly justified by induction on input size or input structure.
Unsynchronized shared mutable state can create race conditions because observed behavior depends on execution interleaving order.
A consistent global lock ordering can prevent deadlock by breaking the circular-wait condition.
Amdahl's law shows that the serial fraction of a workload limits the maximum speedup obtainable from parallelization.
Asymptotic complexity and real performance are connected but not identical because constants, memory locality, and parallel overhead all matter in practice.
Program assurance spans a spectrum from testing for confidence to formal reasoning for proof, so verification and testing are related but distinct tools.
Reductions transfer hardness insight from one problem to another, connecting problem relationships to complexity classification.
Reasoning about concurrent programs requires both logical correctness arguments and systems knowledge about interleavings, races, and deadlock conditions.
